Name: jacobian_layer_skipping
Title: Training-Free Latency Optimization via Jacobian-Guided Layer Skipping
Short Hypothesis: The Jacobian of hidden state transitions can serve as a zero-overhead proxy for layer importance, enabling dynamic layer skipping without compromising inference stability under strict latency budgets.
Related Work: Recent works like Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation focus on activation geometry and PCA-based filtering for latency-aware inference. However, these methods rely on activation subspaces rather than layer-specific dynamics. Our proposal uniquely leverages the Jacobian of hidden state transitions, which provides a direct measure of layer importance and perturbation sensitivity, enabling more precise layer skipping.
Abstract: Strict latency budgets in real-world machine learning deployments often necessitate dynamic compute reduction, such as layer skipping. However, existing methods rely on heuristic or activation-based criteria, which can lead to unstable inference under distribution shifts. We propose a training-free framework that uses the Jacobian of hidden state transitions to dynamically skip layers while preserving inference stability. By computing the Jacobian norm during the forward pass, we identify layers that contribute minimally to the final prediction and skip them when latency thresholds are exceeded. We theoretically ground our approach in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking Jacobian norms to perturbation stability. Experiments on single-GPU setups across adversarial benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers compared to activation-based baselines. By replacing heuristic layer skipping with mathematically transparent Jacobian diagnostics, we provide a reproducible pathway for latency-aware inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Evaluate on adversarial benchmarks (e.g., WILDS) and measure throughput-accuracy trade-offs.
Compare against activation-based layer skipping baselines (e.g., PCA filtering, spectral routing).
Measure inference stability under controlled distribution shifts using stability radii.
Quantify computational overhead of Jacobian computation vs. baseline methods.
Validate theoretical PAC-Bayes bounds on perturbation stability using Jacobian norms.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Jacobian computation may introduce overhead, though we hypothesize it will be negligible.
The method assumes smooth hidden state transitions, which may not hold for all architectures.
Layer skipping may degrade performance on tasks requiring deep hierarchical reasoning.
Theoretical bounds rely on assumptions about perturbation distributions, which may not generalize.
